First line: Hello, World!
phase1.explanation:
Buffered streams improve performance by reducing disk I/O:

BufferedReader:
• Wraps a Reader (FileReader, etc.)
• Reads chunks of data into a buffer
• Provides convenient methods like readLine()

BufferedWriter:
• Wraps a Writer
• Writes in chunks, reducing disk operations
• Provides newLine() for platform-independent newlines

Why buffering improves performance:
• Without buffering: each operation = disk access (slow)
• With buffering: operations go to memory (fast), then disk once

Benefits of BufferedReader:
• readLine() — reads entire lines
• Much faster than FileReader.read()
• Handles different line endings

Benefits of BufferedWriter:
• newLine() — writes appropriate line separator
• Faster for multiple writes

Wrapping pattern:
BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new FileReader("file.txt"));

Pattern: wrap to add functionality
• Reader → BufferedReader (adds buffering + readLine)
• Writer → BufferedWriter (adds buffering + newLine)

phase2.errorMessage:
Exception in thread "main" java.lang.NullPointerException: Cannot invoke "String.length()" because "line" is null
    at Test.main(Test.java:9)

phase3.prompt:
Write a program that:
1. Uses BufferedWriter to write 5 lines to a file called "poem.txt"
2. Lines: any 5 lines of your choice
3. Uses BufferedReader to read the file back
4. Prints each line with a line number prefix: "1: [line]"

Use try-with-resources for both operations.
dialogueHints: BufferedWriter: write() and newLine() for each line
BufferedReader: readLine() in while loop until null
Track line number with a counter
